message.content:
In the repo at S:\01_Repos\BeFirst_Portal, I need to understand:
1. Run `git log --oneline -20` to see recent commit history
2. Check what areas/modules have been recently modified (git log --name-only -10)
3. Look at the current git status for pending changes
4. Check if there are any documentation-related files already in this repo (README, docs folder, etc)

This will help understand what kind of changes need to be documented.

content.text: I'll help you understand the recent changes in the BeFirst Portal repository. Let me gather that information.

1→# Portal
     2→
     3→
     4→
     5→## Getting started
     6→
     7→To make it easy for you to get started with GitLab, here's a list of recommended next steps.
     8→
     9→Already a pro? Just edit this README.md and make it your own. Want to make it easy? [Use the template at the bottom](#editing-this-readme)!
    10→
    11→## Add your files
    12→
    13→- [ ] [Create](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/repository/web_editor.html#create-a-file) or [upload](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/repository/web_editor.html#upload-a-file) files
    14→- [ ] [Add files using the command line](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/gitlab-basics/add-file.html#add-a-file-using-the-command-line) or push an existing Git repository with the following command:
    15→
    16→```
    17→cd existing_repo
    18→git remote add origin https://gitlab.lcx.app/lcx-le/application/befirst/portal.git
    19→git branch -M main
    20→git push -uf origin main
    21→```
    22→
    23→## Integrate with your tools
    24→
    25→- [ ] [Set up project integrations](https://gitlab.lcx.app/lcx-le/application/befirst/portal/-/settings/integrations)
    26→
    27→## Collaborate with your team
    28→
    29→- [ ] [Invite team members and collaborators](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/members/)
    30→- [ ] [Create a new merge request](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/merge_requests/creating_merge_requests.html)
    31→- [ ] [Automatically close issues from merge requests](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/issues/managing_issues.html#closing-issues-automatically)
    32→- [ ] [Enable merge request approvals](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/merge_requests/approvals/)
    33→- [ ] [Set auto-merge](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/project/merge_requests/merge_when_pipeline_succeeds.html)
    34→
    35→## Test and Deploy
    36→
    37→Use the built-in continuous integration in GitLab.
    38→
    39→- [ ] [Get started with GitLab CI/CD](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/ci/quick_start/index.html)
    40→- [ ] [Analyze your code for known vulnerabilities with Static Application Security Testing (SAST)](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/application_security/sast/)
    41→- [ ] [Deploy to Kubernetes, Amazon EC2, or Amazon ECS using Auto Deploy](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/topics/autodevops/requirements.html)
    42→- [ ] [Use pull-based deployments for improved Kubernetes management](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/clusters/agent/)
    43→- [ ] [Set up protected environments](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/ci/environments/protected_environments.html)
    44→
    45→***
    46→
    47→# Editing this README
    48→
    49→When you're ready to make this README your own, just edit this file and use the handy template below (or feel free to structure it however you want - this is just a starting point!). Thanks to [makeareadme.com](https://www.makeareadme.com/) for this template.
    50→
    51→## Suggestions for a good README
    52→
    53→Every project is different, so consider which of these sections apply to yours. The sections used in the template are suggestions for most open source projects. Also keep in mind that while a README can be too long and detailed, too long is better than too short. If you think your README is too long, consider utilizing another form of documentation rather than cutting out information.
    54→
    55→## Name
    56→Choose a self-explaining name for your project.
    57→
    58→## Description
    59→Let people know what your project can do specifically. Provide context and add a link to any reference visitors might be unfamiliar with. A list of Features or a Background subsection can also be added here. If there are alternatives to your project, this is a good place to list differentiating factors.
    60→
    61→## Badges
    62→On some READMEs, you may see small images that convey metadata, such as whether or not all the tests are passing for the project. You can use Shields to add some to your README. Many services also have instructions for adding a badge.
    63→
    64→## Visuals
    65→Depending on what you are making, it can be a good idea to include screenshots or even a video (you'll frequently see GIFs rather than actual videos). Tools like ttygif can help, but check out Asciinema for a more sophisticated method.
    66→
    67→## Installation
    68→Within a particular ecosystem, there may be a common way of installing things, such as using Yarn, NuGet, or Homebrew. However, consider the possibility that whoever is reading your README is a novice and would like more guidance. Listing specific steps helps remove ambiguity and gets people to using your project as quickly as possible. If it only runs in a specific context like a particular programming language version or operating system or has dependencies that have to be installed manually, also add a Requirements subsection.
    69→
    70→## Usage
    71→Use examples liberally, and show the expected output if you can. It's helpful to have inline the smallest example of usage that you can demonstrate, while providing links to more sophisticated examples if they are too long to reasonably include in the README.
    72→
    73→## Support
    74→Tell people where they can go to for help. It can be any combination of an issue tracker, a chat room, an email address, etc.
    75→
    76→## Roadmap
    77→If you have ideas for releases in the future, it is a good idea to list them in the README.
    78→
    79→## Contributing
    80→State if you are open to contributions and what your requirements are for accepting them.
    81→
    82→For people who want to make changes to your project, it's helpful to have some documentation on how to get started. Perhaps there is a script that they should run or some environment variables that they need to set. Make these steps explicit. These instructions could also be useful to your future self.
    83→
    84→You can also document commands to lint the code or run tests. These steps help to ensure high code quality and reduce the likelihood that the changes inadvertently break something. Having instructions for running tests is especially helpful if it requires external setup, such as starting a Selenium server for testing in a browser.
    85→
    86→## Authors and acknowledgment
    87→Show your appreciation to those who have contributed to the project.
    88→
    89→## License
    90→For open source projects, say how it is licensed.
    91→
    92→## Project status
    93→If you have run out of energy or time for your project, put a note at the top of the README saying that development has slowed down or stopped completely. Someone may choose to fork your project or volunteer to step in as a maintainer or owner, allowing your project to keep going. You can also make an explicit request for maintainers.
    94→

1→# firstronic
     2→
     3→> A Vue.js project
     4→
     5→## Build Setup
     6→
     7→``` bash
     8→# install dependencies
     9→npm install
    10→
    11→# serve with hot reload at localhost:8080
    12→npm run dev
    13→
    14→# build for production with minification
    15→npm run build
    16→```
    17→
    18→For detailed explanation on how things work, consult the [docs for vue-loader](http://vuejs.github.io/vue-loader).
    19→

1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## Project Overview
     6→
     7→**BeFirst Portal** is an ASP.NET MVC 5 manufacturing portal (.NET Framework 4.8) for tracking production, quality, materials, dashboards, and other factory floor operations. It integrates heavily with the **PLEX ERP** system via SOAP APIs.
     8→
     9→- **Solution**: `Portal_BeFirst.sln`
    10→- **Main project**: `firstronic/Portal_BeFirst.csproj`
    11→- **Assembly/Namespace**: `Firstronic`
    12→- **Source control**: GitLab (`gitlab.lcx.app`)
    13→
    14→## Build & Run
    15→
    16→This is a classic .NET Framework 4.8 project. It does **not** support `dotnet build` or `dotnet run`.
    17→
    18→```bash
    19→# Build via MSBuild (Visual Studio Developer Command Prompt)
    20→msbuild firstronic/Portal_BeFirst.csproj /p:Configuration=Debug
    21→
    22→# Or open Portal_BeFirst.sln in Visual Studio and build (F5 to run with IIS Express)
    23→```
    24→
    25→There are no unit tests in this project. There is no linting or formatting tool configured.
    26→
    27→## Required Environment Variables
    28→
    29→All configuration is via environment variables (nothing in Web.config). These must be set before running:
    30→
    31→| Variable | Purpose |
    32→|----------|---------|
    33→| `BeFirst:ConnectionString` | Main SQL Server database |
    34→| `BeFirst:ConnectionString_STG` | Staging database |
    35→| `BeFirst:ConnectionString_ODS` | Operational data store |
    36→| `BeFirst:ConnectionString_DWH` | Data warehouse |
    37→| `BeFirst:ConnectionStringMachines` | Machine data |
    38→| `BeFirst:ConnectionStringHanwha` | Hanwha SMT machines |
    39→| `BeFirst:PLEXSOAPUsername/Password` | PLEX ERP SOAP credentials |
    40→| `BeFirst:APIUrl/APIUser/APIPassword` | Internal API |
    41→| `BeFirst:SMTPEmail/SMTPPassword` | Email sending |
    42→| `BeFirst:FilesIP` | Network file storage |
    43→
    44→Full list in `firstronic/Framework/GlobalVariables/GlobalVariables.cs`.
    45→
    46→## Architecture
    47→
    48→### Areas Pattern (Primary Organization)
    49→
    50→The app is organized into **18 MVC Areas** under `firstronic/Areas/`. Each area has its own `Controllers/`, `Models/`, `Views/`, and `{Name}AreaRegistration.cs`:
    51→
    52→| Area | Domain |
    53→|------|--------|
    54→| **Dashboards** | TV displays, 3D floor layout (Three.js), OEE, downtime, packout charts |
    55→| **Production** | Packout goals, shortages, rework, reports, PLEX transaction reconciliation |
    56→| **Quality** | CAPAS audits, ESD audits, 5S audits, first piece inspection, defects, scrap |
    57→| **Materials** | Warehouse ops, kanban, tool crib, MSL reset, receiving, shortages |
    58→| **Engineering** | Part numbers, setups, rates, tools, feeders, stencils |
    59→| **Tracking** | Unit history, container movements, BOM used, serial tracking, road map |
    60→| **Manufacturing** | OEE, SMT reports (SPI, AOI, pick & place), solder paste |
    61→| **Manage** | Stations, areas, cells, users, roles, settings, agents, extractors |
    62→| **Planning** | Production planning tool, balance, job hints |
    63→| **Tickets** | Internal ticketing system with dashboards |
    64→| **Personnel** | Teams/bonus, visitors, QA user locks |
    65→| **Mobile** | Mobile-optimized interfaces (SMT) |
    66→| **Rework** | Rework tracking |
    67→| **Testing** | Test operations |
    68→| **Purchasing** | Procurement |
    69→| **IS/IT** | Information systems, trainings |
    70→| **Utils** | Printers |
    71→
    72→### Framework Layer (`firstronic/Framework/`)
    73→
    74→Custom infrastructure providing cross-cutting concerns:
    75→
    76→- **`BaseController.cs`** - All controllers inherit from this. Provides language/culture selection (cookie-based), notification helpers (Success/Error/Info/Warning), error handling
    77→- **`BePlex.cs`** - Large (480KB) PLEX ERP SOAP integration. Production data, BOMs, work orders
    78→- **`Data/`** - Dual database context approach:
    79→  - `LegacyDataContext/DataContext.cs` - EF6 `IdentityDbContext` for ASP.NET Identity and legacy tables
    80→  - `AppDataContext/AppDbContext.cs` - EF Core 3.1 for newer features (PLEX maintenance data)
    81→  - `Repository.cs` / `IRepository.cs` - Generic repository pattern over EF6
    82→- **`Functions/Queries.cs`** - Raw ADO.NET SQL helpers (`GetDatatableFromQuery()`, `GetIntFromQuery()`)
    83→- **`GlobalVariables/GlobalVariables.cs`** - All environment variable configuration
    84→- **`Account/`** - ASP.NET Identity 2.0 (`ApplicationUser` with Badge, ControlID, Location fields)
    85→- **`UI/`** - Custom HTML helpers (`HtmlHelper.Fst()` extensions), `ViewComponentBase`
    86→- **`Cache/`** - `MemoryCacheManager` / `ICacheManager` (singleton)
    87→- **`Languages/`** - Localization via `Settings.resx` (English/Spanish)
    88→- **`APIs/`** - Internal API clients (Station, Materials, Containers, Account)
    89→
    90→### Dependency Injection
    91→
    92→Uses **Autofac** (configured in `App_Start/DependencyRegister.cs`):
    93→- `DataContext` as `IDataContext` (per-request)
    94→- `AppDbContext` (per-request)
    95→- `MemoryCacheManager` as `ICacheManager` (singleton)
    96→- `ApplicationUserService` for Identity
    97→- `Engine.cs` provides service locator via `Engine.Resolve<T>()`
    98→
    99→### Authentication
   100→
   101→Cookie-based via OWIN middleware (`App_Start/Startup.Auth.cs`). ASP.NET Identity 2.0 with custom `ApplicationUser` extending `IdentityUser`.
   102→
   103→### Frontend
   104→
   105→- **Razor views** (~1800 `.cshtml` files) with layouts:
   106→  - `_Layout.cshtml` - Main layout
   107→  - `_Layout_portal.cshtml` - Portal layout
   108→  - `_Layout_tvs_*.cshtml` - TV dashboard layouts (color variants)
   109→- **CSS/JS bundling** via `BundleConfig.cs`
   110→- **Key libraries**: Bootstrap 5.3, jQuery 3.7, Font Awesome 6, Chart.js, DataTables, Select2, TinyMCE, Toastr, Three.js (3D factory layout), D3.js
   111→- Static assets in `firstronic/Content/` (css, img, vendor libraries)
   112→
   113→### Navigation
   114→
   115→Menu structure is built dynamically in `Models/Menus/MenuIndex.cs` using database queries for areas/stations. Uses custom `Nav`/`Dropdown` classes from `Framework/UI/`.
   116→
   117→## Key Conventions
   118→
   119→- Controllers use raw SQL via `Queries.GetDatatableFromQuery()` for read-heavy operations and the `Repository<T>` pattern for CRUD
   120→- Localization strings are in `Framework/Languages/Settings.resx` (accessed via `Resources.Menu.*`, `Resources.Settings.*`)
   121→- New services should be registered in `App_Start/DependencyRegister.cs`
   122→- Database entities extend `Framework/Entities/BaseEntity`
   123→- File uploads go to `firstronic/ExcelUploads/` and `firstronic/UploadedFiles/`
   124→- Session timeout is 60 minutes (Web.config)
   125→- Max upload size is 1GB (Web.config `maxRequestLength`)
   126→
   127→## Git Workflow
   128→
   129→- **Primary branch**: `develop` (active development)
   130→- **Main branch**: `master`
   131→- Commit message prefixes used: `STYLE:`, `DELETE:`, `FIX`, `Fix:`
   132→

content.text:
## Summary of Changes in BeFirst Portal Repository

Based on my analysis of the repository at `S:\01_Repos\BeFirst_Portal`, here's what I found:

### 1. Recent Commit History (Last 20 Commits)

The repository shows active development focused on:
- **Rebranding efforts** - Most recent commit is about rebranding home pages
- **Module cleanup** - Large deletion of non-manufacturing modules (Customs, Finances, Continual Improvement, Instructions, Management)
- **Bug fixes** - Various fixes for placements, material movements, shortages
- **Features** - Dashboard enhancements, status changes, audit fixes, solder paste validation

### 2. Recently Modified Areas/Modules (Last 10 Commits)

The most active areas are:
- **Continual Improvement Area** - Completely removed (MoonShine, Suggestions, Reports controllers)
- **Customs Area** - Completely removed (Exportation, Logistics, Movements controllers)
- **Finances Area** - Completely removed (UnitsTreatTime, Portal controllers)
- **Instructions Area** - Completely removed (Editor, Media, Status controllers)
- **Quality Area** - Partial cleanup (BackTrack, various reports)
- **Production, Dashboards, Engineering** - Minor modifications

### 3. Current Git Status (Pending Changes)

There are **14 modified/deleted files** not yet committed:

**Rebranding changes:**
- `Logo.cs` - Changed from Lacroix logos to Safari Circuits logos
- `Footer.cshtml` - Changed site name from "LACROIX NA" to "SAFARI CIRCUITS"
- `_Layout_portal.cshtml` - Layout updates
- `Settings.resx` and `Settings.Designer.cs` - Localization updates
- `style.css` - Minor CSS adjustments

**Module cleanup:**
- `BackTrackController.cs` (deleted) - 855 lines removed
- `ContinualImprovementController.cs` (deleted) - 250 lines removed
- `MenuIndex.cs` - Removed BackTrack and problem tracking menu items (15 lines changed)
- `Portal_BeFirst.csproj` - Project file updates reflecting deletions

**Other changes:**
- `DowntimeController.cs`, `AssemblyInstructionsController.cs`, `ShortagesController.cs` - Minor modifications
- `Index.cshtml` - Removed JUA1 and JUA2 server links

**New files (untracked):**
- `CLAUDE.md` - AI coding assistant guidance (already exists, good!)
- Safari Circuits logo files (3 PNG variants + background image)

### 4. Existing Documentation

The repository has **minimal documentation**:

**Root level:**
- `README.md` - Generic GitLab template (not customized for this project)
- `CLAUDE.md` - **Excellent project documentation** specifically for AI assistants, covering architecture, conventions, build process

**Firstronic folder:**
- `firstronic/README.md` - Outdated Vue.js boilerplate (not relevant to this ASP.NET MVC project)

**Other READMEs:**
- Various vendor library READMEs in `packages/` and `Content/vendor/` folders (not project-specific)

### Key Insights

1. **Major rebranding underway**: The project is transitioning from Lacroix branding to Safari Circuits
2. **Significant module removal**: Non-manufacturing modules are being systematically removed (~1,137 lines deleted)
3. **CLAUDE.md is excellent**: This file provides comprehensive project guidance and should be the model for other documentation
4. **README.md needs updating**: The root README is just a GitLab template and should be replaced with actual project documentation
5. **No docs folder**: There's no dedicated documentation directory for technical specs, deployment guides, or user documentation

### Recommendations for Documentation

Given these changes, you should document:
1. **Rebranding guide** - What changed, what logos to use, naming conventions
2. **Module removal rationale** - Why these areas were removed, what functionality was deprecated
3. **Updated README.md** - Replace GitLab template with actual project overview (using CLAUDE.md as a reference)
4. **Deployment guide** - How to set up environment variables, build, and deploy
5. **Architecture decision records (ADRs)** - Document why dual EF contexts exist, why certain areas were removed

All file paths provided are absolute paths as shown above.
